pick.rationale: Kentucky holds a narrow statistical edge with a 7-spot KenPom advantage and superior defensive profile that should help contain Santa Clara's elite offense. While the Broncos present a credible upset threat through their top-15 ranked offense, elite offensive rebounding, and three-point shooting that targets Kentucky's perimeter weaknesses, the Wildcats' SEC-tested balance and more efficient two-way metrics provide a thin edge in this closely contested 7/10 matchup.

reasoningStep.title: Kentucky vs Santa Clara 7/10 Statistical Evaluation
reasoningStep.summary: Analyzed both teams' advanced metrics revealing an extremely close matchup with only 7 spots separating them in KenPom rankings. Kentucky shows superior defensive balance while Santa Clara relies on an elite offense but weaker defense. Santa Clara has a credible upset path through offensive rebounding and three-point shooting, but Kentucky's SEC pedigree and more efficient two-way profile provide a narrow edge.
evidence: Kentucky ratings: KenPom #28, Torvik #32, +21.47 EM, #39 offense, #32 defense, 21-13 record
Santa Clara ratings: KenPom #35, Torvik #28, +19.42 EM, #23 offense, #76 defense, 26-8 record
Recent form: Kentucky lost to Florida in SEC Tournament; Santa Clara lost to Gonzaga 79-68 in WCC Championship after beating Saint Mary's
Matchup dynamics: Santa Clara's offensive rebounding and three-point shooting aligns with Kentucky's perimeter defensive weaknesses; Kentucky's superior defense provides containment advantage
Betting market: Kentucky -1.5 to -3.5 favorite indicating slight edge

result.text: Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket - The Athletic Premier League Champions League 2026 Men's World Cup F1 Tennis FA Cup Cricket La Liga Europa League Women's Football Olympics Global Sports Cycling Sailing Sports Business Boxing Golf Serie A Bundesliga International Football NFL NBA MLB World Baseball Classic NHL MLS Transfer News FPL League Cup Championship League One League Two Women's Euros NWSL College Sports NCAAF NCAAM Peak MMA Motorsports NASCAR Culture Memorabilia & Collectibles Gaming Sports Betting The PL Is Being Left Behind Welcome to Chelsea, Liam Rosenior PL Player of the Year Nominees Log In Subscribe Now Tickets by Viagogo Connections: Sports Edition REGION U.S. International Search Top News Podcasts NCAAM Bracket Scores & Schedule Teams Alabama Arizona Arkansas Duke Florida Gonzaga Houston Illinois Iowa State Kansas Louisville Miami (FL) Michigan State Michigan Nebraska North Carolina Purdue Saint Mary's St. John's Tennessee Texas Tech UConn Vanderbilt Virginia Wisconsin Standings Beat Our Expert's Bracket Tournament Projections Printable Bracket Predictions Game Men's March Madness LIVE First Four Updates Tournament Forecast Challenge Our Experts The Perfect Bracket Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key Injuries To Know Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

result.text: Kentucky basketball NCAA Tournament foe Santa Clara: An early look Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball SEC Sports Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports An early look at Kentucky NCAA Tournament foe Santa Clara Vance Meek UK Wildcats Wire The Kentucky Wildcats now know their NCAA Tournament path, and it begins on Friday in the Midwest Region. The Cats are the No. 7 seed in that bracket and will face No. 10 seed Santa Clara to open up difficult road to the Final Four. Looking ahead would be foolish, however. Santa Clara doesn't come from a major conference, but they are a very good team and will provide a difficult test for Kentucky. The Broncos are 26-8 this season and made it to the West Coast Conference championship game. Santa Clara's strengths match up well with Kentucky's weaknesses. The Broncos are one of the best teams in the country at getting offensive rebounds. They feature plenty of size in the frontcourt, including 7'1" center Bukky Oboye. They also shoot a lot of three pointers, something the Wildcats haven't always defended well. Kentucky's defense will need to be strong, especially on the perimeter. Christian Hammond can score quickly, and knock down shots. Freshman Allen Graves has also been terrific. Otega Oweh and Denzel Aberdeen will need to match their intensity. It figures to be a fun game with plenty of shooting from deep. Tip off is scheduled for 12:15 P.M. ET on Friday. result.text: Gonzaga 79-68 Santa Clara (Mar 10, 2026) Game Recap - ESPN Skip to main content Skip to navigation ESPN NFL NFL NBA NBA NCAAM NCAAM NCAAW NCAAW MLB MLB NHL NHL Soccer Soccer More Sports More Sports Watch Watch Fantasy Fantasy Where to Watch Search Gonzaga Bulldogs vs Santa Clara Broncos West Coast Conference Tournament - Final Santa Clara Broncos Santa Clara SCU 26-8 , 15-3 WCC 68 26-8 , 15-3 WCC Final 1 2 T Santa Clara SCU 33 35 68 Gonzaga GONZ 29 50 79 12 Gonzaga Bulldogs Gonzaga GONZ 30-3 , 16-2 WCC 79 30-3 , 16-2 WCC Gamecast Recap Box Score Play-by-Play Team Stats Videos No. 12 Gonzaga beats Santa Clara 79-68 to win the WCC tournament in its final appearance By AP Updated: Mar 11, 2026, 12:51 am Facebook Facebook Messenger Twitter Email LAS VEGAS -- — Gonzaga has dominated the West Coast Conference since coach Mark Few took over at the turn of the century. The Bulldogs are leaving for the Pac-12 Conference next season and made sure to go out winners. Mario Saint-Supéry scored 21 points, Tyon Grant-Foster had 20 and No. 12 Gonzaga defeated Santa Clara 79-68 on Tuesday night to win the WCC Tournament. “It's a special feeling to go out the right way,” said Gonzaga's Graham Ike , voted the tournament Most Outstanding Player. “Ultimately, we ended where we started this. It started out with championships and those teams that came before us. We just wanted to continue the success that they had. We were proud we could do that.” The Bulldogs (30-3) received the conference's automatic bid to the NCAA Tournament, though the only mystery was what seed they will get. This was their sixth WCC tournament title in seven years and 12th in the last 14. Santa Clara (26-8) will wait until Sunday to see if it is in the 68-team field, but the WCC appears to be a three-bid league. Gonzaga, Santa Clara and No. 21 Saint Mary's were in the top 38 of the NCAA's NET rankings entering this game and in the top 35 in Kenpom. Four players scored in double figures for the Zags, who made 52.8% of their shots. Ike made all seven of his shots in scoring 15 points. Christian Hammond led the Broncos with 24 points, and Allen Graves scored 11. Sash Gavalyugov , who scored 23 points that included the dagger 3-pointer in the closing seconds to beat Saint Mary's 76-71 in the semifinals, was held to eight. He was 3 of 13, including 2 of 9 from 3-point range. “Sash had some tough shots tonight,” Santa Clara coach Herb Sendek said. “He does have the ability to make some of those shots, but I don't know that things were engineered to simply take him out.” The Broncos made Gonzaga earn this victory. They led 52-50 nearly midway through the second half before the Bulldogs scored seven straight points to go ahead for good. After dominating the WCC, Gonzaga will see if it can continue to assert itself against San Diego State, Boise State and Utah State in the revamped Pac-12 beginning next season. That will come later. Gonzaga has yet another NCAA Tournament appearance to place its focus. “

result.text: Otega Oweh is 'gonna be fine' after hard fall while Lamont Butler is a 'minute-by-minute' deal - On3 Skip to main content KSR KSR Plus KSBoard Login Mobile Menu Home KSR+ KSBoard Basketball News Feed Roster Schedule Transfer Portal Ranking Transfer Portal KSR Draft History Cats in NBA Football News Feed Roster Schedule Transfer Portal Ranking Transfer Portal KSR Draft History Cats in NFL BB Recruiting BB Recruiting Search Commitments Transfers Team Rankings Player Rankings Industry Comparison Offers Visits Recruiting Prediction Machine FB Recruiting FB Recruiting Search Commitments Transfers Team Rankings Player Rankings Industry Comparison Offers Visits Recruiting Prediction Machine Other UK Sports Women's Basketball Volleyball Baseball Softball More KSR Preps KSR Podcasts Funkhouser Youtube KSR APP Shop KSBAR and GRILLE NIL NIL NIL U NIL Rankings NIL 100 NIL Collectives Sports Business About About Staff Newsletter Text Alerts Advertise Contact On3 Teams On3 Kentucky Join KSR+ Otega Oweh is 'gonna be fine' after hard fall while Lamont Butler is a 'minute-by-minute' deal by: Jack Pilgrim 01/30/25 Read In App Big Blue Nation let out the loudest collective gasp in the history of mankind when Otega Oweh hit the deck following a tough baseline dunk late in the second half in Knoxville. He got caught on the rim, flipping his legs up and sending him to the floor face- and shoulder-first. In typical Tega-Tron fashion, though, the junior guard bounced back up unfazed by the vicious fall and finished the game. Tough as nails, sure, but still human. Like anyone would be in his spot, Mark Pope says Oweh is pretty sore after the spill, but will ultimately be fine — hurt, not injured. Just needs a couple of days to heal those bumps and bruises before suiting back up against Arkansas. “Sometimes you have to temper your practice. We have some guys that will go lighter (Thursday) than normal, just because they went through it yesterday. If you saw the game, Otega Oweh came down really, really hard, and bounced off the floor all the way back onto his feet. It was so violent,” Pope said during his call-in show Wednesday night . “He’s got a little bit of soreness — he’s actually gonna be fine, he’s healthy, but he’s gonna deal with some things.” Where do things stand with Lamont Butler , who is currently dealing with a bruised shoulder? He was declared out the night before the Tennessee game, but said on Instagram Live afterward he’d ‘be back soon’ — much better than the ‘if and when we get him back, we’re gonna be elated’ verbiage Pope shared during the postgame press conference. Not much to share a day removed from the win. “As of today — like, when I left the office at 5:50 (PM), Lamont is not good to go tonight. We’ll see how he is in the morning,” Pope said. “We take it minute-by-minute. I told you I was checking in every five minutes, every media timeout to see if I could get him for the next media timeout last gam

Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

result.text: Kentucky basketball NCAA Tournament foe Santa Clara: An early look Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball SEC Sports Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports An early look at Kentucky NCAA Tournament foe Santa Clara Vance Meek UK Wildcats Wire The Kentucky Wildcats now know their NCAA Tournament path, and it begins on Friday in the Midwest Region. The Cats are the No. 7 seed in that bracket and will face No. 10 seed Santa Clara to open up difficult road to the Final Four. Looking ahead would be foolish, however. Santa Clara doesn't come from a major conference, but they are a very good team and will provide a difficult test for Kentucky. The Broncos are 26-8 this season and made it to the West Coast Conference championship game. Santa Clara's strengths match up well with Kentucky's weaknesses. The Broncos are one of the best teams in the country at getting offensive rebounds. They feature plenty of size in the frontcourt, including 7'1" center Bukky Oboye. They also shoot a lot of three pointers, something the Wildcats haven't always defended well. Kentucky's defense will need to be strong, especially on the perimeter. Christian Hammond can score quickly, and knock down shots. Freshman Allen Graves has also been terrific. Otega Oweh and Denzel Aberdeen will need to match their intensity. It figures to be a fun game with plenty of shooting from deep. Tip off is scheduled for 12:15 P.M. ET on Friday. Gonzaga 79-68 Santa Clara (Mar 10, 2026) Game Recap - ESPN Skip to main content Skip to navigation ESPN NFL NFL NBA NBA NCAAM NCAAM NCAAW NCAAW MLB MLB NHL NHL Soccer Soccer More Sports More Sports Watch Watch Fantasy Fantasy Where to Watch Search Gonzaga Bulldogs vs Santa Clara Broncos West Coast Conference Tournament - Final Santa Clara Broncos Santa Clara SCU 26-8 , 15-3 WCC 68 26-8 , 15-3 WCC Final 1 2 T Santa Clara SCU 33 35 68 Gonzaga GONZ 29 50 79 12 Gonzaga Bulldogs Gonzaga GONZ 30-3 , 16-2 WCC 79 30-3 , 16-2 WCC Gamecast Recap Box Score Play-by-Play Team Stats Videos No. 12 Gonzaga beats Santa Clara 79-68 to win the WCC tournament in its final appearance By AP Updated: Mar 11, 2026, 12:51 am Facebook Facebook Messenger Twitter Email LAS VEGAS -- — Gonzaga has dominated the West Coast Conference since coach Mark Few took over at the turn of the century. The Bulldogs are leaving for the Pac-12 Conference next season and made sure to go out winners. Mario Saint-Supéry scored 21 points, Tyon Grant-Foster had 20 and No. 12 Gonzaga defeated Santa Clara 79-68 on Tuesday night to win the WCC Tournament. “It's a special feeling to go out the right way,” said Gonzaga's Graham Ike , voted the tournament Most Outstanding Player. “Ultimately, we ended where we started this. It started out with championships and those teams that came before us. We just wanted to continue the success that they had. We were proud we could do that.” The Bulldogs (30-3) received the conference's automatic bid to the NCAA Tournament, though the only mystery was what seed they will get. This was their sixth WCC tournament title in seven years and 12th in the last 14. Santa Clara (26-8) will wait until Sunday to see if it is in the 68-team field, but the WCC appears to be a three-bid league. Gonzaga, Santa Clara and No. 21 Saint Mary's were in the top 38 of the NCAA's NET rankings entering this game and in the top 35 in Kenpom. Four players scored in double figures for the Zags, who made 52.8% of their shots. Ike made all seven of his shots in scoring 15 points. Christian Hammond led the Broncos with 24 points, and Allen Graves scored 11. Sash Gavalyugov , who scored 23 points that included the dagger 3-pointer in the closing seconds to beat Saint Mary's 76-71 in the semifinals, was held to eight. He was 3 of 13, including 2 of 9 from 3-point range. “Sash had some tough shots tonight,” Santa Clara coach Herb Sendek said. “He does have the ability to make some of those shots, but I don't know that things were engineered to simply take him out.” The Broncos made Gonzaga earn this victory. They led 52-50 nearly midway through the second half before the Bulldogs scored seven straight points to go ahead for good. After dominating the WCC, Gonzaga will see if it can continue to assert itself against San Diego State, Boise State and Utah State in the revamped Pac-12 beginning next season. That will come later. Gonzaga has yet another NCAA Tournament appearance to place its focus. “

Given the close statistical profile and that 7/10 games are historically near coin flips, I should consider whether Santa Clara has a credible upset path. They do:

- Impressive WCC tournament performance, including a quality win against Saint Mary's
- Comparable efficiency metrics that suggest competitive potential

I'm leaning toward Kentucky but with modest confidence. The statistical evidence supports a tight contest where small factors could determine the outcome. The Wildcats' defensive consistency and SEC experience provide a slight edge in a matchup that could easily go either way.

result.text: Otega Oweh is 'gonna be fine' after hard fall while Lamont Butler is a 'minute-by-minute' deal - On3 Skip to main content KSR KSR Plus KSBoard Login Mobile Menu Home KSR+ KSBoard Basketball News Feed Roster Schedule Transfer Portal Ranking Transfer Portal KSR Draft History Cats in NBA Football News Feed Roster Schedule Transfer Portal Ranking Transfer Portal KSR Draft History Cats in NFL BB Recruiting BB Recruiting Search Commitments Transfers Team Rankings Player Rankings Industry Comparison Offers Visits Recruiting Prediction Machine FB Recruiting FB Recruiting Search Commitments Transfers Team Rankings Player Rankings Industry Comparison Offers Visits Recruiting Prediction Machine Other UK Sports Women's Basketball Volleyball Baseball Softball More KSR Preps KSR Podcasts Funkhouser Youtube KSR APP Shop KSBAR and GRILLE NIL NIL NIL U NIL Rankings NIL 100 NIL Collectives Sports Business About About Staff Newsletter Text Alerts Advertise Contact On3 Teams On3 Kentucky Join KSR+ Otega Oweh is 'gonna be fine' after hard fall while Lamont Butler is a 'minute-by-minute' deal by: Jack Pilgrim 01/30/25 Read In App Big Blue Nation let out the loudest collective gasp in the history of mankind when Otega Oweh hit the deck following a tough baseline dunk late in the second half in Knoxville. He got caught on the rim, flipping his legs up and sending him to the floor face- and shoulder-first. In typical Tega-Tron fashion, though, the junior guard bounced back up unfazed by the vicious fall and finished the game. Tough as nails, sure, but still human. Like anyone would be in his spot, Mark Pope says Oweh is pretty sore after the spill, but will ultimately be fine — hurt, not injured. Just needs a couple of days to heal those bumps and bruises before suiting back up against Arkansas. “Sometimes you have to temper your practice. We have some guys that will go lighter (Thursday) than normal, just because they went through it yesterday. If you saw the game, Otega Oweh came down really, really hard, and bounced off the floor all the way back onto his feet. It was so violent,” Pope said during his call-in show Wednesday night . “He’s got a little bit of soreness — he’s actually gonna be fine, he’s healthy, but he’s gonna deal with some things.” Where do things stand with Lamont Butler , who is currently dealing with a bruised shoulder? He was declared out the night before the Tennessee game, but said on Instagram Live afterward he’d ‘be back soon’ — much better than the ‘if and when we get him back, we’re gonna be elated’ verbiage Pope shared during the postgame press conference. Not much to share a day removed from the win. “As of today — like, when I left the office at 5:50 (PM), Lamont is not good to go tonight. We’ll see how he is in the morning,” Pope said. “We take it minute-by-minute. The page is truncated. I have gathered enough information to make a decision. Let me synthesize the data:

**Summary:**

1. **Statistical Profile (Very Close):**
   - Kentucky: KenPom #28, Torvik #32, +21.47 EM, 21-13 record
   - Santa Clara: KenPom #35, Torvik #28, +19.42 EM, 26-8 record
   - Gap: Only 7 spots in KenPom, essentially identical

2. **Team Strengths:**
   - Kentucky: More balanced (#39 offense, #32 defense), SEC-tested
   - Santa Clara: Better offense (#15 Torvik, #23 KenPom), elite offensive rebounding, three-point shooting, but weaker defense (#76)

3. **Recent Form:**
   - Kentucky: Beat LSU 87-82, lost to Florida 71-63 in SEC Tournament
   - Santa Clara: Beat Saint Mary's 76-71, lost to Gonzaga 79-68 in WCC Championship (quality performance)

4. **Matchup Dynamics:**
   - Santa Clara's strengths (offensive rebounding, three-point shooting) align with Kentucky's weaknesses (perimeter defense)
   - Kentucky's defense is significantly better than Santa Clara's
   - Betting market has Kentucky as slight favorite (-1.5 to -3.5)

5. **Injuries:**
   - No major confirmed injuries for either team entering the tournament

This is a very close 7/10 matchup. Historically, these games are near coin flips. The statistical profile is almost identical, with Santa Clara having a slightly better offense and Kentucky having a better defense. Santa Clara has a credible upset path through their elite offensive rebounding and three-point shooting.
 
The SEC's competitive landscape gives Kentucky a slight advantage, despite their underwhelming record. Their defensive capabilities and previous exposure to high-stakes games provide a narrow edge. While Santa Clara's offensive prowess is notable, the Wildcats' tournament experience and defensive tenacity ultimately tip the scales in a tightly contested matchup.

Based on my analysis, this is one of the closest matchups in the bracket with remarkably similar statistical profiles. Kentucky holds a narrow 7-spot edge in KenPom rankings (#28 vs #35) with a +21.47 efficiency margin against Santa Clara's +19.42. The Wildcats feature a more balanced attack with the #39 offense and #32 defense, while Santa Clara relies on an elite offense (#15 Torvik, #23 KenPom) but carries a porous #76-ranked defense. The Broncos present a credible upset threat through their elite offensive rebounding and three-point shooting that directly targets Kentucky's perimeter defensive weaknesses, and their WCC tournament run—including a quality win over Saint Mary's and competitive loss to Gonzaga—proves they can compete with high-major competition. However, Kentucky's SEC-tested resume, superior defensive profile, and more efficient two-way balance provide a narrow but reliable edge in this classic 7/10 coin-flip scenario.
